revision.comment: Bot: remove [[Category:Sn crud]] (crud category cleanup) [[github:EmmaLeonhart/shintowiki-scripts/actions/runs/23991619004|time triggered pipeline]]
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Imizu-jinja]][[en:Imizu Shrine]][[ja:射水神社]][[zh:射水神社]] {{Short description|Shinto Shrine}} [[en:Imizu Shrine]] [[en:Imizu Shrine]][[ja:射水神社]][[zh:射水神社]] [[en:Imizu Shrine]] [[ja:射水神社]] [[zh:射水神社]] [[en:Imizu Shrine]][[ja:射水神社]][[zh:射水神社]] {{Short description|Shinto Shrine}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Imizu Shrine |native_name = 射水神社 | image = File:Imizu-jinja haiden.JPG | caption = ''Haiden'' of Imizu Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Toyama Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|36.748528|N|137.021333|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = {{ill|Ninigi|qid=Q1056017|1=Ninigi-no-Mikoto}} | founded_by = | established = pre-{{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|qid=Q189178|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period}} | date_destroyed = | location = {{ill|Takaoka|qid=Q502988|1=Takaoka, Toyama}} | website = {{Official|1=https://www.imizujinjya.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'' | festival = April 23 }} {{nihongo|'''Imizu Shrine'''|射水神社|Imizu Jinja}} is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|qid=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located in the Kojo neighborhood of the city of {{ill|Takaoka, Toyama|en|Takaoka, Toyama|qid=Q502988|lt=Takaoka|1=Takaoka, Toyama}}, {{ill|Toyama Prefecture|en|Toyama Prefecture|qid=Q132929|lt=Toyama Prefecture|1=Toyama Prefecture}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. It one of four shrines claiming the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former {{ill|Etchū Province|en|Etchū Province|qid=Q906899|lt=Etchū Province|1=Etchū Province}}. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 23.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}}</ref>
== Enshrined ''kami'' == The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Imizu Jinja is: * {{nihongo|'''Futagami-no-kami'''|二上神||}}, which is identified with [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto|Ninigi-no-mikoto]], and the ancestral spirit of the Imizu ''[[kuni no miyatsuko]]''
==History== The foundation of shrine is uncertain, but is believed to be from before the {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|qid=Q189178|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period}}. According to shrine legend, Mount Futagami was worshipped as a {{ill|Sacred mountains|en|Sacred mountains|qid=Q1595289|lt=Sacred mountains|1=Sacred mountains}} since ancient times. In 717 AD, the wandering monk {{ill|Gyōki|en|Gyōki|qid=Q1136664|lt=Gyōki|1=Gyōki}} visited the area, and built the temple of Yōrō-ji at the base of the mountain to subsume the mountain cult into Buddhism under ''[[shinbutsu-shūgō]]''. The shrine first appears in documentary records in the 780 AD ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'', where it is recorded as having received the court Rank equivalent of Lower 5th Rank, Junior grade. It was promoted to Lower 5th Rank Senior grade per the 795 AD ''{{ill|Nihon Kōki|en|Nihon Kōki|qid=Q1077029|lt=Nihon Kōki|1=Nihon Kōki}}'' and to Senior 4th Rank senior grade per the 840 AD ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]''. In 854 AD, it was granted Junior 3rd Rank ''[[Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku]]'', and in 859 AD advanced to Senior 3rd Rank per the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]''.The name of the shrine appears in a poem by {{ill|Ōtomo no Yakamochi|en|Ōtomo no Yakamochi|qid=Q386308|lt=Ōtomo no Yakamochi|1=Ōtomo no Yakamochi}} in the ''{{ill|Man'yōshū|en|Man'yōshū|qid=Q834321|lt=Man'yōshū|1=Man'yōshū}}'' poetry anthology, which is another indication of its fame and status. Per the 927 AD ''[[Engishiki]]'', the shrine is listed as the largest shrine is Izumi County of {{ill|Etchū Province|en|Etchū Province|qid=Q906899|lt=Etchū Province|1=Etchū Province}}. The shrine was initially regarded as the ''Ni-no-miya'' of Etchū Province, and became the ''ichinomiya'' of the province when {{ill|Noto Province|en|Noto Province|qid=Q1047671|lt=Noto Province|1=Noto Province}} was separated from Etchū, with the former ''ichinomiya'', [[Keta Taisha]], falling within the borders of Noto Province. The shrine burned down during the {{ill|Jōhei|en|Jōhei|qid=Q1324874|lt=Jōhei|1=Jōhei}} era (931-937) AD, and never regained its former prominence. It was rebuilt in 1475 and destroyed again in the {{ill|Sengoku period|en|Sengoku period|qid=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period|1=Sengoku period}}. After the establishment of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]], it was patronized by the [[Maeda clan]] and ancient ''[[yamabushi]]'' rituals were revived.<ref name="Yoshiki">{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}}</ref>
Following the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]], the shrine was deprived of its revenues under the ''[[shinbutsu bunri]]'' decrees and its Buddhist facilities quickly fell into ruin. With the establishment of {{ill|State Shinto|en|State Shinto|qid=Q1197248|lt=State Shinto|1=State Shinto}}, the shrine was designated as a {{nihongo|National shrine, 2nd Rank|国幣中社||[[:en:Kokuhei-chūsha|Kokuhei-chūsha]]}}. The shrine was relocated to its present location within the former second bailey of {{ill|Takaoka Castle|en|Takaoka Castle|qid=Q8011764|lt=Takaoka Castle|1=Takaoka Castle}} in 1875 – a move greatly opposed by the inhabitants of its former location, who were angered that the government was depriving them of their ancient place of worship. <ref name="Yoshiki"/>
The new shrine was destroyed in June 1900 during the Great Takaoka Fire, and was rebuilt in 1902. The current ''[[Shinmei-zukuri]]''-style [[Honden]] dates from this time.
The shrine is located a 15-minute walk from {{ill|Takaoka Station (Toyama)|en|Takaoka Station (Toyama)|qid=Q871547|lt=Takaoka Station|1=Takaoka Station (Toyama)}}.<ref name="Okada">{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}}</ref>
==Gallery== <gallery> File:Imizu jinja Gate.JPG|Torii: File:国幣中社射水神社.jpg|Imizu Shrine in 1938 File:明治期の国幣中社射水神社.jpg|Imizu Shrine in 1909 </gallery>
==See also== *{{ill|List of Shinto shrines|en|List of Shinto shrines|qid=Q120679573|lt=List of Shinto shrines|1=List of Shinto shrines}} *[[Ichinomiya]] *Other shrines claiming to be {{ill|Etchū Province|en|Etchū Province|qid=Q906899|lt=Etchū|1=Etchū Province}} Ichinomiya ** [[Keta Jinja]] ** [[Takase Shrine]] ** [[Oyama Shrine (Tateyama)|Oyama Shrine]]
==References== {{reflist}}
==External links== {{commons category|Imizu-jinja}} *[http://www.imizujinjya.or.jp Official website] *[https://www.info-toyama.com/spot/21131/ Toyama Tourist Information]
